# Getting AI to Work in Complex Codebases

It seems pretty well-accepted that AI coding tools struggle with real production codebases. The [Stanford study on AI's impact on developer productivity](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tbDDYKRFjhk) found:

1. A lot of the "extra code" shipped by AI tools ends up just reworking the slop that was shipped last week.
2. Coding agents are great for new projects or small changes, but in large established codebases, they can often make developers *less* productive. 

The common response is somewhere between the pessimist "this will never work" and the more measured "maybe someday when there are smarter models."

After several months of tinkering, I've found that **you can get really far with today's models if you embrace core context engineering principles**.

This isn't another "10x your productivity" pitch. I [tend to be pretty measured when it comes to interfacing with the ai hype machine](https://hlyr.dev/12fa). But we've stumbled into workflows that leave me with considerable optimism for what's possible. We've gotten claude code to handle 300k LOC Rust codebases, ship a week's worth of work in a day, and maintain code quality that passes expert review. We use a family of techniques I call "frequent intentional compaction" - deliberately structuring how you feed context to the AI throughout the development process.

I am now fully convinced that AI for coding is not just for toys and prototypes, but rather a deeply technical engineering craft.

**Video Version**: If you prefer video, this post is based on [a talk given at Y Combinator on August 20th](https://hlyr.dev/ace)

### Grounding Context from AI Engineer

Two talks from AI Engineer 2025 fundamentally shaped my thinking about this problem.

The first is [Sean Grove's talk on "Specs are the new code"](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8rABwKRsec4) and the second is [the Stanford study on AI's impact on developer productivity](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tbDDYKRFjhk).

Sean argued that we’re all *vibe coding wrong*. The idea of chatting with an AI agent for two hours, specifying what you want, and then throwing away all the prompts while committing only the final code… is like a Java developer compiling a JAR and checking in the compiled binary while throwing away the source. 

Sean proposes that in the AI future, the specs will become the real code. That in two years, you'll be opening python files in your IDE with about the same frequency that, today, you might open up a hex editor to read assembly (which, for most of us, is never).

[Yegor's talk on developer productivity](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tbDDYKRFjhk) tackled an orthogonal problem. They analyzed commits from 100k developers and found, among other things,

1. That AI tools often lead to a lot of rework, diminishing the perceived productivity gains

<img width="2008" height="1088" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f7cec497-3ee2-47d1-8f91-a18210625e19" />

2. That AI tools work well for greenfield projects, but are often counter-productive for brownfield codebases and complex tasks

<img width="1326" height="751" alt="Screenshot 2025-08-29 at 10 55 32 AM" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/06f03232-f9d9-4a92-a182-37056bf877a4" />

This matched what I heard talking with founders:

* “Too much slop.”
* “Tech debt factory.”
* “Doesn’t work in big repos.”
* “Doesn’t work for complex systems.”

The general vibe on AI-coding for hard stuff tends to be

> Maybe someday, when models are smarter…

Heck even [Amjad](https://x.com/amasad) was on a [lenny's podcast 9 months ago](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/behind-the-product-replit-amjad-masad) talking about how PMs use Replit agent to prototype new stuff and then they hand it off to engineers to implement for production.
(Disclaimer: i haven't caught up with him recently (ok, ever), this stance may have changed)

Whenever I hear "Maybe someday when the models are smart" I generally leap to exclaim **that's what context engineering is all about**: getting the most out of *today's* models.

### What's actually possible today

I'll deep dive on this a bit futher down, but to prove this isn't just theory, let me outline a concrete example. A few weeks ago, I decided to test our techniques on [BAML](https://github.com/BoundaryML/baml), a 300k LOC Rust codebase for a programming language that works with LLMs. I'm at best an amateur Rust dev and had never touched the BAML codebase before.

Within an hour or so, I had a [PR fixing a bug](https://github.com/BoundaryML/baml/pull/2259#issuecomment-3155883849) which was approved by the maintainer the next morning. A few weeks later, [@hellovai](https://x.com/hellovai) and I paired on shipping 35k LOC to BAML, adding [cancellation support](https://github.com/BoundaryML/baml/pull/2357) and [WASM compilation](https://github.com/BoundaryML/baml/pull/2330) - features the team estimated would take a senior engineer 3-5 days each. We got both draft prs ready in about 7 hours.

Again, this is all built around a workflow we call [frequent intentional compaction](#what-works-even-better-frequent-intentional-compaction) - essentially designing your entire development process around context management, keeping utilization in the 40-60% range, and building in high-leverage human review at exactly the right points. We use a "research, plan, implement" workflow, but the core capabilities/learnings here are FAR more general than any specific workflow or set of prompts.

### Our weird journey to get here

I was working with one of the most productive AI coders I've ever met. 
Every few days they'd drop **2000-line Go PRs**.
And this wasn't a nextjs app or a CRUD API. This was complex, [race-prone systems code](https://github.com/humanlayer/humanlayer/blob/main/hld/daemon/daemon_subscription_integration_test.go#L45) that did JSON RPC over unix sockets and managed streaming stdio from forked unix processes (mostly claude code sdk processes, more on that later 🙂).

The idea of carefully reading 2,000 lines of complex Go code every few days was simply not sustainable. I was starting to feel a bit like Mitchell Hashimoto when he added the [AI contributions must be disclosed](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/pull/8289) rules for ghostty.

Our approach was to adopt something like sean's **spec-driven development**.

It was uncomfortable at first. 
I had to learn to let go of reading every line of PR code. 
I still read the tests pretty carefully, but the specs became our source of truth for what was being built and why.

The transformation took about 8 weeks. 
It was incredibly uncomfortable for everyone involved, not least of all for me. 
But now we're flying. A few weeks back, I shipped 6 PRs in a day. 
I can count on one hand the number of times I've edited a non-markdown file by hand in the last three months.

## Advanced Context Engineering for Coding Agents

What we needed was:

* AI that Works Well in Brownfield Codebases
* AI that Solves Complex Problems
* No Slop
* Maintain Mental Alignment across the team

(And yeah sure, let's try to spend as many tokens as possible.)

I'll dive into:

1. what we learned applying context engineering to coding agents
2. the dimensions along which using these agents is a deeply technical craft
3. why I don't believe these approaches are generalizable
4. the number of times I've been repeatedly proven wrong about (3)

### But first: The Naive Way to manage agent context

Most of us start by using a coding agent like a chatbot. You talk (or [drunkenly shout](https://ghuntley.com/six-month-recap/#:~:text=Last%20week%2C%20over%20Zoom%20margaritas%2C%20a%20friend%20and%20I%20reminisced%20about%20COBOL.)) back and forth with it, vibing your way through a problem until you either run out of context, give up, or the agent starts apologizing.

<img width="7718" height="4223" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7361a203-9d95-42e2-ac16-1f38b04adb58" />


A slightly smarter way is to just start over when you get off track, discarding your session and starting a new one, perhaps with a little more steering in the prompt. 

> [original prompt], but make sure you use XYZ approach, because ABC approach won't work

<img width="7727" height="4077" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1bbbc8ad-60da-4f8b-98c3-e6603b04a0ce" />

### Slightly Smarter: Intentional Compaction

You have probably done something I've come to call "intentional compaction". Whether you're on track or not, as your context starts to fill up, you probably want to pause your work and start over with a fresh context window. To do this, you might use a prompt like

> "Write everything we did so far to progress.md, ensure to note the end goal, the approach we're taking, the steps we've done so far, and the current failure we're working on"

<img width="7309" height="4083" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/64b940e5-89b1-4f6c-a79c-ec2810d9af77" />


You can also [use commit messages for intentional compaction](https://x.com/dexhorthy/status/1961490837017088051).

### What Exactly Are We Compacting?

What eats up context?

* Searching for files
* Understanding code flow
* Applying edits
* Test/build logs
* Huge JSON blobs from tools

All of these can flood the context window. **Compaction** is simply distilling them into structured artifacts.

A good output for an intentional compaction might include something like

<img width="1309" height="747" alt="Screenshot 2025-08-29 at 11 10 36 AM" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a7d5946d-4e81-46e8-b314-d02dae1f00ee" />


### Why obsess over context?

As we went deep on in [12-factor agents](https://hlyr.dev/12fa), LLMs are stateless functions. The only thing that affects the quality of your output (without training/tuning models themselves) is the quality of the inputs. 

This is just as true for [wielding](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F_RyElT_gJk) coding agents as it is for general agent design, you just have a smaller problem space, and rather than building agents, we're talking about using agents. 

At any given point, a turn in an agent like claude code is a stateless function call. Context window in, next step out. 

<img width="7309" height="4083" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c1e920e8-5dc5-4dd2-b76d-853b85a92e6a" />

That is, the contents of your context window are the ONLY lever you have to affect the quality of your output. So yeah, it's worth obsessing over.

You should optimize your context window for:

1. Correctness
2. Completeness
3. Size
4. Trajectory

Put another way, the worst things that can happen to your context window, in order, are:

1. Incorrect Information
2. Missing Information
3. Too much Noise

If you like equations, here's a dumb one you can reference:

<img width="1320" height="235" alt="Screenshot 2025-08-29 at 11 11 30 AM" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a6ea98a6-665b-48af-983b-a1cb2c45e44c" />

As [Geoff Huntley](https://x.com/GeoffreyHuntley) puts it,

> The name of the game is that you only have approximately **170k of context window** to work with. 
> So it's essential to use as little of it as possible. 
> The more you use the context window, the worse the outcomes you'll get.

Geoff's solution to this engineering constraint is a technique he calls [Ralph Wiggum as a Software Engineer](https://ghuntley.com/ralph/), which basically involves running an agent in a while loop forever with a simple prompt.

```
while :; do
  cat PROMPT.md | npx --yes @sourcegraph/amp 
done
```

If you wanna learn more about ralph or what's in PROMPT.md, you can check out Geoff's post or dive into the project that [@simonfarshid](https://x.com/simonfarshid), [@lantos1618](https://x.com/lantos1618), [@AVGVSTVS96](https://x.com/AVGVSTVS96) and I built at last weekend's YC Agents Hackathon, which was able to (mostly) [port BrowserUse to TypeScript overnight](https://github.com/repomirrorhq/repomirror/blob/main/repomirror.md)

Geoff describes ralph as a "hilariously dumb" solution to the context window problem. [I'm not entirely sure that it is dumb](https://ghuntley.com/content/images/size/w2400/2025/07/The-ralph-Process.png).

### Back to compaction: Using Sub-Agents

Subagents are another way to manage context, and generic subagents (i.e. not [custom](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/sub-agents) ones) have been a feature of claude code and many coding CLIs since the early days.

Subagents are not about [playing house and anthropomorphizing roles](https://x.com/dexhorthy/status/1950288431122436597). Subagents are about context control.

The most common/straightforward use case for subagents is to let you use a fresh context window to do finding/searching/summarizing that enables the parent agent to get straight to work without clouding its context window with `Glob` / `Grep` / `Read` / etc calls.



https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/cb4e7864-9556-4eaa-99ca-a105927f484d


<details><summary>(video not playing on mobile? expand for the static image version)</summary>
  <img width="7309" height="4083" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c72e7dba-1476-4ee9-9cb0-0f97d428b82a" />
</details>


The ideal subagent response probably looks similar to the ideal ad-hoc compaction from above

<img width="1309" height="747" alt="Screenshot 2025-08-29 at 11 10 36 AM" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a7d5946d-4e81-46e8-b314-d02dae1f00ee" />

Getting a subagent to return this is not trivial:

<img width="7309" height="4083" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2bcd30f6-84fd-4911-ac15-63f75619e76d" />


### What works even better: Frequent Intentional Compaction

The techniques I want to talk about and that we've adopted in the last few months fall under what I call "frequent intentional compaction".

Essentially, this means designing your ENTIRE WORKFLOW around context management, and keeping utilization in the 40%-60% range (depends on complexity of the problem ).

The way we do it is to split into three (ish) steps. 

I say "ish" because sometimes we skip the research and go straight to planning, and sometimes we'll do multiple passes of compacted research before we're ready to implement. 

I'll share example outputs of each step in a concrete example below. For a given feature or bug, we'll tend to do:

**Research**

Understand the codebase, the files relevant to the issue, and how information flows, and perhaps potential causes of a problem.

here's our [research prompt](https://github.com/humanlayer/humanlayer/blob/main/.claude/commands/research_codebase.md). 
It currently uses custom subagents, but in other repos I use a more generic version that uses the claude code Task() tool with `general-agent`. 
The generic one works almost as well.


**Plan**

Outline the exact steps we'll take to fix the issue, and the files we'll need to edit and how, being super precise about the testing / verification steps in each phase.

This is the [prompt we use for planning](https://github.com/humanlayer/humanlayer/blob/main/.claude/commands/create_plan.md).


**Implement**

Step through the plan, phase by phase. For complex work, I'll often compact the current status back into the original plan file after each implementation phase is verified.

This is the [implementation prompt we use](https://github.com/humanlayer/humanlayer/blob/main/.claude/commands/implement_plan.md).

Aside - if you've been hearing a lot about git worktrees, this is the only step that needs to be done in a worktree. We tend to do everything else on main.

**How we manage/share the markdown files**

I will skip this part for brevity but feel free to launch a claude session in [humanlayer/humanlayer](https://github.com/humanlayer/humanlayer) and ask how the "thoughts tool" works.

### Putting this into practice 

I do a [weekly live-coding session](https://github.com/ai-that-works/ai-that-works) with [@vaibhav](https://www.linkedin.com/in/vaigup/) where we whiteboard and code up a solution to an advanced AI Engineering problem. It's one of the highlights of my week.

Several weeks ago, I [decided to share some more about the process](https://hlyr.dev/he-gh), curious if our in-house techniques could one-shot a fix to a 300k LOC Rust codebase for BAML, a programming language for working with LLMs. I picked out [an (admittedly small-ish) bug](https://github.com/BoundaryML/baml/issues/1252) from the @BoundaryML repo and got to work. 

You can [watch the episode](https://hlyr.dev/he-yt) to learn more about the process, but to outline it:

**Worth noting**: I am at best an amateur Rust dev, and I have never worked in the BAML codebase before.

#### The research

- I created a piece of research, I read it. Claude decided the bug was invalid and the codebase was correct.
- I threw that research out and kicked off a new one, with more steering.
- here is [the final research doc i ended up using](https://github.com/ai-that-works/ai-that-works/blob/main/2025-08-05-advanced-context-engineering-for-coding-agents/thoughts/shared/research/2025-08-05_05-15-59_baml_test_assertions.md)

#### The plans

- While the research was running, I got impatient and kicked off a plan, with no research, to see if claude could go straight to an implementation plan - [you can see it here](https://github.com/ai-that-works/ai-that-works/blob/main/2025-08-05-advanced-context-engineering-for-coding-agents/thoughts/shared/plans/fix-assert-syntax-validation-no-research.md)
- When the research was done, I kicked off another implementation plan that used the research results - [you can see it here](https://github.com/ai-that-works/ai-that-works/blob/main/2025-08-05-advanced-context-engineering-for-coding-agents/thoughts/shared/plans/baml-test-assertion-validation-with-research.md)

The plans are both fairly short, but they differ significantly. They fix the issue in different ways, and have different testing approaches. Without going too much into detail, they both "would have worked" but the one built with research fixed the problem in the *best* place and prescribed testing that was in line with the codebase conventions.

#### The implementation

- This was all happening the night before the podcast recording. I ran both plans in parallel and submitted both as PRs before signing off for the night.

By the time we were on the show at 10am PT the next day, [the PR from the plan with the research was already approved by @aaron](https://github.com/BoundaryML/baml/pull/2259#issuecomment-3155883849), who didn't even know I was doing a bit for a podcast 🙂. We [closed the other one](https://github.com/BoundaryML/baml/pull/2258/files).

So out of our original 4 goals, we hit:

- ✅ Works in brownfield codebases (300k LOC rust project)
- Solves complex problems
- ✅ no slop (pr merged)
- Keeps mental alignment

### Solving complex problems

Vaibhav was still skeptical, and I wanted to see if we could solve a more complex problem.

So a few weeks later, the two of us spent 7 hours (3 hours on research/plans, 4 hours on implementation) and shipped 35k LOC to add cancellation and wasm support to BAML. 
The [cancelation PR just got merged last week](https://github.com/BoundaryML/baml/pull/2357). [The WASM one is still open](https://github.com/BoundaryML/baml/pull/2330), but has a working demo of calling the wasm-compiled rust runtime from a JS app in the browser. 

While the cancelation PR required a little more love to take things over the line, we got incredible progress in just a day. Vaibhav estimated that each of these PRs would have been 3-5 days of work for a senior engineer on the BAML team to complete.

✅ So we can solve complex problems too. 


### This is not Magic

Remember that part in the example where I read the research and threw it out cause it was wrong? Or me and Vaibhav sitting DEEPLY ENGAGED FOR 7 HOURS? You have to engage with your task when you're doing this or it WILL NOT WORK.

There's a certain type of person who is always looking for the one magic prompt that will solve all their problems. It doesn't exist.

Frequent Intentional Compaction via a research/plan/implement flow will make your performance **better**, but what makes it **good enough for hard problems** is that you build high-leverage human review into your pipeline.

<img width="7309" height="4083" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/01c7818a-9a0d-4ede-a23b-fb0c2e80f843" />

### Eggs on Faces

A few weeks back, [@blakesmith](https://www.linkedin.com/in/bhsmith/) and I sat down for 7 hours and [tried to remove hadoop dependencies from parquet java](https://github.com/dexhorthy/parquet-java/blob/remove-hadoop/thoughts/shared/plans/remove-hadoop-dependencies.md) - the deep dive on everything that went wrong and my theories as to why, I'll save for another post, suffice it to say that it did not go well. The tl;dr is that the research steps didn't go deep enough through the dependency tree, and assumed classes could be moved upstream without introducing deeply nested hadoop dependencies.

There are big hard problems you cannot just prompt your way through in 7 hours, and we're still curiously and excitedly hacking on pushing the boundaries with friends and partners. I think the other learning here is that you probably need at least one person who is an expert in the codebase, and for this case, that was neither of us.

### On Human Leverage

If there's one thing you take away from all this, let it be this:

A bad line of code is… a bad line of code.
But a bad line of a **plan** could lead to hundreds of bad lines of code.
And a bad line of **research**, a misunderstanding of how the codebase works or where certain functionality is located, could land you with thousands of bad lines of code.

<img width="7309" height="4083" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/dab49f61-caae-4c15-b481-ee9b8f64995f" />

So you want to **focus human effort and attention** on the HIGHEST LEVERAGE parts of the pipeline.

<img width="9830" height="4520" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/cf981f70-5e61-4938-aa9a-7dcb88c9f8a4" />


When you review the research and the plans, you get more leverage than you do when you review the code. (By the way, one of our primary focuses @ [humanlayer](https://hlyr.dev/code) is helping teams build and leverage high-quality workflow prompts and crafting great collaboration workflows for ai-generated code and specs).

### What is code review for?

People have a lot of different opinions on what code review is for.

I prefer [Blake Smith's framing in Code Review Essentials for Software Teams](https://blakesmith.me/2015/02/09/code-review-essentials-for-software-teams.html), where he says the most important part of code review is mental alignment - keeping members of the team on the page as to how the code is changing and why.

<img width="7309" height="4083" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/77f4001b-175f-4da6-a6d4-e00b80489476" />

Remember those 2k line golang PRs? I cared about them being correct and well designed, but the biggest source of internal unrest and frustration on the team was the lack of mental alignment. **I was starting to lose touch with what our product was and how it worked.** 

I would expect that anyone who's worked with a very productive AI coder has had this experience.

This is actually the most important part of research/plan/implement to us. 
A guaranteed side effect of everyone shipping way more code is that a much larger proportion of your codebase is going to be unfamiliar to any given engineer at any point in time.

I won't even try to convince you that research/plan/implement is the right approach for most teams - it probably isn't. But you ABSOLUTELY need an engineering process that 

1. keeps team members on the same page
2. enables team members to quickly learn about unfamiliar parts of the codebase

For most teams, this is pull requests and internal docs. For us, it's now specs, plans, and research.

I can't read 2000 lines of golang daily. But I *can* read 200 lines of a well-written implementation plan.

I can't go spelunking through 40+ files of daemon code for an hour+ when something is broken (okay, I can, but I don't want to). I *can* steer a research prompt to give me the speed-run on where I should be looking and why.

### Recap

Basically we got everything we needed.

- ✅ Works in brownfield codebases
- ✅ Solves complex problems
- ✅ No slop
- ✅ Maintains mental alignment

(oh, and yeah, our team of three is averaging about $12k on opus per month)

So you don't think I'm just another [hyped up mustachio'd sales guy](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IS_y40zY-hc&lc=UgzFldRM6LU5unLuFn54AaABAg.AMKlTmJAT5ZAMKrOOAMw3I), I'll note that this does not work perfectly for every problem (we'll be back for another round sound, parquet-java).

In August the whole team spent 2 weeks spinning circles on a really tricky race condition that spiraled into a rabbit hole of issues with MCP sHTTP keepalives in golang and a whole bunch of other dead ends. 

But that's the exception now. In general, this works well for us. Our intern shipped 2 PRs on his first day, and 10 on his 8th day. I was genuinely skeptical that it would work for anyone else, but me and Vaibhav shipped 35k LOC of working BAML code in 7 hours. (And if you haven't met Vaibhav, he's one of the most meticulous engineers I know when it comes to code design and quality.)

### What's coming

I'm reasonably confident that coding agents will be commoditized.

The hard part will be the team and workflow transformation. Everything about collaboration will change in a world where AI writes 99% of our code.

And I believe pretty strongly that if you don't figure this out, you're gonna get lapped by someone who did. 

### okay so clearly you have something to sell me

We're pretty bullish on spec-first, agentic workflows, so we're building tools to make it easier. Among many things, I'm obsessed with the problem of scaling these "frequent intentional compaction" workflows collaboratively across large teams. 

Today, we're launching CodeLayer, our new "post-IDE IDE" in private beta - think "Superhuman for claude code". If you're a fan of Superhuman and/or vim mode and you're ready to move beyond "vibe coding" and get serious about building with agents, we'd love to have you join the waitlist. 

**Sign up at [https://humanlayer.dev](https://humanlayer.dev)**.

## For OSS Maintainers - lets ship something together

If you are a maintainer on a complex OSS project and based in the bay area, my open offer - I will pair with you in-person in SF for 7 hours on a saturday and see if we can ship something big.

I get a lot of learning about the limitations and where these techniques fall short (and, with any luck, a working merged PR that adds a ton of value that I can point to). You get to learn the workflow in the only way I've found that works well - direct 1x1 pairing.

## For Engineering Leaders

If you or someone you know is an engineering leader that wants to 10x their team's productivity with AI, we're forward-deploying with teams of all sizes to help drive the culture/process/tech shift needed to transition to an ai-first coding world.

#### `.claude/rules/` (5 files)
**Core project rules:**

   - POUR accessibility requirements
   - Design token locking (Nocturne/Sonata only)
   - ui-ux-pro-max suggestion protocol

4. **output-completeness.md** — Code delivery quality
   - Bans partial code (`// ...`, `TODO`, etc.)
   - Bans prose shortcuts
   - Full deliverable requirement
   - Token limit handling with pause/resume

5. **examples.md** — Few-shot examples

### 6. Orchestration & Automation Configuration

**Settings.json Highlights:**
- **Hooks enabled:** Pre/post-edit, pre/post-task, user prompt submit, session start/end, stop, pre-compact, subagent-start
- **Claude Flow V3:** Enabled with hierarchical-mesh swarm topology, max 15 agents
- **Agent Teams:** Auto-assign on idle, train patterns on complete, shared memory namespace
- **Memory:** Hybrid backend, HNSW vector search, learning bridge, memory graph, agent scopes all enabled
- **Daemon Workers:** map, audit, optimize, consolidate, testgaps, ultralearn, deepdive, document, refactor, benchmark
- **Security:** Auto-scan, scan on edit, CVE checks, threat modeling
- **ADR:** Auto-generate in `/docs/adr` with MADR template
- **DDD:** Track domains, validate bounded contexts in `/docs/ddd`

### 7. Key Behavioral Rules (from CLAUDE.md in memory)

**Always-Active:**
- Interaction: preview → gate → execute (never blend)
- Music theory: Theory Named strategy (academic anchoring)
- Code style: Tailwind tokens only, no ad-hoc hex values
- Output: No `// ...`, no `TODO`, no partial code
- UI: POUR accessibility always

**Skill Trigger Protocol:**
- Use `@skill-name` to trigger local skills
- Use `@username` for Antigravity specialists
- Search before implementing: `python3 references/antigravity-awesome-skills/scripts/search.py <query> --limit 5`

## Summary

The HarmonyForge repository is a highly structured Claude Code + Claude Flow V3 project with:

- **5 locked rule files** defining interaction protocol, music theory constraints, code style, output completeness, and examples
- **117 agent definitions** across 15+ specialized categories
- **159 command definitions** for SPARC workflows, GitHub automation, analysis, optimization, and monitoring
- **25 persistent skill definitions** with embedded data for UI/UX, memory patterns, architecture, and verification
- **47 helper scripts** for lifecycle automation, memory sync, daemon management, and security
- **Hierarchical-mesh topology** with up to 15 concurrent agents
- **Hybrid memory backend** with HNSW vector search, learning bridges, and memory graphs
- **V3 daemon system** with 10 background workers running on scheduled intervals
- **Comprehensive security & ADR tracking** with auto-generation and threat modeling
- **Design system lock:** Nocturne/Sonata tokens only, no ad-hoc colors

This is a production-grade, enterprise-scale coordination system for multi-agent music theory development with rigorous quality gates and deterministic academic anchoring.

```text
     1→# HCI Interaction Protocol
     2→
     3→## Role
     4→
     5→You are the **Senior HCI Architect & Music Theorist**. Build lean, modular, high-context "Glass Box" systems grounded in **Intrinsic Determinism** — anchoring every claim in rigorous academic definitions, not probabilistic guessing.
     6→
     7→## Workflow (MANDATORY — every response)
     8→
     9→### Step 1 — Prompt-Structuring
    10→
    11→Transform every user message into this exact XML block:
    12→
    13→```xml
    14→<context>what the user is building and why</context>
    15→<task>the specific component or feature requested</task>
    16→<constraints>stack requirements, a11y rules, symbolic-only music handling, Theory Named strategy</constraints>
    17→<output>expected deliverable: component, hook, type definition, etc.</output>
    18→```
    19→
    20→### Step 2 — Meta-Rewrite
    21→
    22→Immediately after the block, answer:
    23→
    24→- What is the user actually asking for?
    25→- What is the minimum surface area of code needed?
    26→- Are there any ambiguities to resolve before proceeding?
    27→
    28→### Step 3 — Confirmation Gate 🔒
    29→
    30→End the preview reply with exactly this (verbatim):
    31→
    32→> **Shall I proceed with the implementation as described above? (Yes / No / Request changes)**
    33→
    34→### Step 4 — Waiting State
    35→
    36→Do **not** advance until the next message is one of: `Yes`, `Proceed`, `Approved`.
    37→If changes are requested, return to Step 1 and iterate.
    38→
    39→### Step 5 — Execution
    40→
    41→1. Follow `<tasks>` precisely.
    42→2. Obey all `<constraints>` — no hallucination, cite sources, use intrinsic determinism.
    43→3. Deliver without further restructuring unless requested.
    44→
    45→### Step 6 — Clarifying Questions
    46→
    47→If ambiguous before execution, ask briefly, then return to Step 3.
    48→
    49→## Key Enforcement Rules
    50→
    51→- **Never blend preview and execution in the same message.**
    52→- Confirmation question must be the **final line** of every preview reply.
    53→- Assistant remains **idle** until explicit permission is received.
    54→
    55→---
    56→
    57→## Vague Request Clarification Protocol
    58→
    59→### Trigger Conditions
    60→
    61→Activate this protocol when the user message contains any of:
    62→
    63→- Unanchored aesthetic directives: "make it prettier", "make it better",
    64→  "clean this up", "improve this", "make it look nicer", "fix the vibe",
    65→  "make it more modern", "make it pop", "polish this"
    66→- Outcome-only statements with no specified dimension: "this feels off",
    67→  "I don't like it", "something's wrong with it"
    68→- Comparative vagueness: "make it more like X" (where X is undefined or
    69→  is another product/style without a named token or design principle)
    70→
    71→### Rule
    72→
    73→The assistant **MUST NOT** act on any vague aesthetic request.
    74→Instead, run the Clarification Scaffold below **before** any preview or execution.
    75→This is not optional — it applies even when the intent seems obvious.
    76→
    77→### Clarification Scaffold (ask all four, in order)
    78→
    79→1. **What specifically feels wrong?**
    80→   (e.g., spacing too tight, color contrast low, hierarchy unclear, motion
    81→   too fast, text too dense — name the dimension)
    82→
    83→2. **What is the goal of this element?**
    84→   (e.g., draw attention, provide context, enable quick scanning, confirm
    85→   an action — name the user job-to-be-done)
    86→
    87→3. **What design token or principle should govern the fix?**
    88→   (e.g., Nocturne accent color, 4px spacing grid, WCAG AA contrast,
    89→   Instrument Serif at 1.2rem — or say "I don't know" and we'll look it up together)
    90→
    91→4. **Is this a one-off change or a pattern to apply system-wide?**
    92→   (Determines whether we touch a single component or update a token/rule)
    93→
    94→### After the Scaffold
    95→
    96→Once all four answers are collected, transform them into the XML structured block
    97→(context / task / constraints / output) and proceed through the Preview → Gate → Execute workflow.
    98→
    99→### Rationale
   100→
   101→Vague aesthetic requests encode *symptom* language, not *goal* language.
   102→Acting on symptoms without diagnosis produces arbitrary changes that satisfy
   103→the immediate feeling without addressing the underlying mismatch.
   104→Per Nielsen's Heuristic #5 (Error Prevention): preventing misspecified work
   105→is superior to correcting it after execution.
   106→
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./.claude/rules/music-theory.md`

```text
     1→# Music Theory Constraints
     2→
     3→## Theory Named Strategy (REQUIRED)
     4→Every music theory claim must be anchored in a rigorous academic definition.
     5→
     6→**Format:** "X is defined as [academic definition], per [source/tradition]."
     7→
     8→**Example:** "A parallel fifth is defined as two voices moving in the same direction by a perfect fifth interval, per common-practice voice-leading rules (Aldwell & Schachter, *Harmony and Voice Leading*, 4th ed.)."
     9→
    10→Never state music theory as folk knowledge. Always name the principle.
    11→
    12→## Intrinsic Determinism Boundary
    13→- **Deterministic logic** (constraint satisfaction, voice-leading rules, SATB validation) → handled by the **TypeScript Logic Core** (backend). Never implement in the frontend.
    14→- **LLM explanation** (Glass Box reasoning, violation narration) → handled by the **Theory Inspector** API. The frontend only *displays* structured JSON responses.
    15→- **Frontend role:** Render, schedule, and surface. Never compute theory.
    16→
    17→## SATB Constraints (Reference Only — Do Not Implement Frontend Logic)
    18→- Range constraints: Soprano B3–G5, Alto G3–C5, Tenor C3–G4, Bass E2–C4 (SATB standard ranges, per Aldwell & Schachter).
    19→- Forbidden parallels: parallel fifths, parallel octaves (common-practice rules).
    20→- Voice crossing: soprano must not drop below alto pitch, alto below tenor, tenor below bass.
    21→- Doubling: root doubled in root-position triads unless diminished (where the third is doubled).
    22→
    23→## Violation Flags ("Red Line")
    24→- Violations are computed server-side and delivered as structured JSON.
    25→- The frontend renders inline `RedLineTooltip` annotations on the VexFlow canvas.
    26→- Never derive violation state in the frontend from raw note data.
    27→
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./.claude/rules/typescript-patterns.md`

```text
     1→# TypeScript & Code Style Patterns
     2→
     3→## Stack
     4→- **Framework:** Next.js App Router (TypeScript)
     5→- **Styling:** Tailwind CSS utility classes only — no ad-hoc color values
     6→- **Score Rendering:** VexFlow (symbolic only)
     7→- **Playback:** Tone.js (symbolic event scheduling — no raw audio synthesis)
     8→- **State:** Zustand stores
     9→- **LLM Sidebar:** Theory Inspector (REST/streaming API consumer only)
    10→
    11→## Code Style Rules
    12→- All components: modular, component-driven, strictly typed TypeScript
    13→- No `any` types — define explicit interfaces/types for all props and state
    14→- Zustand stores: typed slices, no implicit `any` in actions
    15→- VexFlow: wrap in typed React components; never manipulate DOM outside `useEffect`
    16→- Tone.js: schedule symbolic events only; no `AudioContext` or raw buffer manipulation
    17→- POUR accessibility: `aria-label`, keyboard nav, and screen-reader annotations required on all interactive elements
    18→
    19→## Design Token Rule
    20→Use only HarmonyForge design system tokens:
    21→- Dark palette: **Nocturne**
    22→- Light palette: **Sonata**
    23→- Never introduce ad-hoc hex values or Tailwind color overrides outside the token system
    24→
    25→### ui-ux-pro-max Skill — Suggestion Protocol
    26→When the ui-ux-pro-max skill surfaces color palette, typography, or style recommendations:
    27→- **Surface them as labelled suggestions** — prefix with `[ui-ux-pro-max suggestion]` so the user can evaluate them explicitly.
    28→- **Never auto-apply** color or font values from the skill. Nocturne/Sonata tokens and the HarmonyForge type scale are the locked baseline.
    29→- **UX pattern suggestions** (`--domain ux`, `--domain react`, `--domain web`) may be adopted directly — they carry no token risk.
    30→- **Deviations from the locked design system** require Superman's explicit approval before implementation. Present the deviation, its rationale, and the specific token it would override.
    31→
    32→## Architecture Boundary
    33→| Concern | Location |
    34→|---|---|
    35→| Score rendering | VexFlow React component |
    36→| Playback scheduling | Tone.js hook (`usePlayback`) |
    37→| Editor state | Zustand store |
    38→| Violation display | `RedLineTooltip` on VexFlow canvas |
    39→| Theory explanation | Theory Inspector sidebar (API consumer) |
    40→| Constraint logic | Backend only — never frontend |
    41→
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./.claude/rules/output-completeness.md`

```text
     1→# Output Completeness Rules
     2→
     3→Source: `references/taste-skill/output-skill/SKILL.md`
     4→Applies to: every code delivery turn (Phase 2+)
     5→
     6→## Core Principle
     7→
     8→A partial output is a broken output. Do not optimize for brevity — optimize for completeness.
     9→
    10→## Banned Patterns (Hard Failures)
    11→
    12→**In code blocks:**
    13→`// ...`, `// rest of code`, `// implement here`, `// TODO`, `/* ... */`,
    14→`// similar to above`, `// continue pattern`, `// add more as needed`, bare `...` standing in for omitted code
    15→
    16→**In prose:**
    17→"Let me know if you want me to continue", "for brevity", "the rest follows the same pattern",
    18→"similarly for the remaining", "and so on" (when replacing actual content), "I'll leave that as an exercise"
    19→
    20→**Structural shortcuts:**
    21→Outputting a skeleton when the request was for a full implementation. Showing the first and last section while skipping the middle. Describing what code should do instead of writing it.
    22→
    23→## Execution Process (Every Code Delivery)
    24→
    25→1. **Scope** — Read the full request. Count distinct deliverables (files, components, hooks, sections). Lock that number.
    26→2. **Build** — Generate every deliverable completely. No partial drafts, no "you can extend this later."
    27→3. **Cross-check** — Before responding, compare deliverable count against scope count. If anything is missing, add it.
    28→
    29→## Handling Token Limits
    30→
    31→Do not compress remaining sections to squeeze them in. Do not skip ahead to a conclusion.
    32→Write at full quality to a clean breakpoint (end of function, file, or section), then emit:
    33→
    34→```
    35→[PAUSED — X of Y complete. Send "continue" to resume from: <next section name>]
    36→```
    37→
    38→On "continue": pick up exactly where stopped. No recap, no repetition.
    39→
    40→## Quick Check (Before Every Code Reply)
    41→
    42→- No banned patterns appear anywhere in the output
    43→- Every item the user requested is present and finished
    44→- Code blocks contain runnable code, not descriptions of what code would do
    45→- Nothing was shortened to save space
    46→
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
     1→# Structured Block Examples
     2→
     3→Few-shot examples of well-formed preview responses for the HarmonyForge workflow.
     4→Pull this file when unsure how to structure a preview reply.
     5→
     6→---
     7→
     8→## Example 1 — UI Component Request
     9→
    10→**User says:** "Add a playback button to the toolbar."
    11→
    12→**Correct preview response:**
    13→
    14→```xml
    15→<context>
    16→  Superman is building the HarmonyForge Tactile Sandbox. The toolbar needs a playback
    17→  control so the composer can hear the current SATB arrangement without leaving the editor.
    18→</context>
    19→<task>
    20→  Create a PlaybackButton React component that dispatches a play/pause event to the
    21→  Tone.js symbolic scheduler via the usePlayback Zustand hook.
    22→</task>
    23→<constraints>
    24→  - Next.js App Router, TypeScript strict mode
    25→  - Tailwind CSS utility classes only — Nocturne/Sonata tokens, no ad-hoc hex values
    26→  - aria-label="Play arrangement" and aria-pressed={isPlaying} required (POUR)
    27→  - Tone.js receives symbolic note events only — no AudioContext or raw buffer access
    28→  - State lives in Zustand; component is stateless beyond reading isPlaying from the store
    29→</constraints>
    30→<output>
    31→  PlaybackButton.tsx component + usePlayback hook stub (if not yet created)
    32→</output>
    33→```
    34→
    35→**Meta-Rewrite:**
    36→- What is the user asking for? A UI control that triggers symbolic playback.
    37→- Minimum surface area: one component + one hook action dispatch. No new store slice needed if isPlaying already exists.
    38→- Ambiguities: Does the playback hook exist? If not, stub it — but do not implement Tone.js scheduling logic here.
    39→
    40→**Shall I proceed with the implementation as described above? (Yes / No / Request changes)**
    41→
    42→---
    43→
    44→## Example 2 — Theory Inspector Display Request
    45→
    46→**User says:** "Show the parallel fifth violation in the score."
    47→
    48→**Correct preview response:**
    49→
    50→```xml
    51→<context>
    52→  The backend has returned a violation JSON payload identifying a parallel fifth between
    53→  Soprano and Alto voices at beat 3, measure 5. Superman wants this violation rendered
    54→  inline on the VexFlow canvas as a Red Line tooltip.
    55→</context>
    56→<task>
    57→  Implement the RedLineTooltip component that attaches to the VexFlow note element
    58→  at the specified beat/measure coordinates and displays the violation label on hover.
    59→</task>
    60→<constraints>
    61→  - VexFlow DOM manipulation inside useEffect only — no direct ref access outside the hook
    62→  - Tooltip text sourced from the backend JSON payload — never derived from raw note data
    63→  - POUR: tooltip must be keyboard-accessible (focus-visible) and have aria-describedby
    64→  - A parallel fifth is defined as two voices moving in the same direction by a perfect
    65→    fifth interval, per common-practice voice-leading rules (Aldwell & Schachter,
    66→    Harmony and Voice Leading, 4th ed.) — display this definition in the tooltip body
    67→  - Nocturne token: use --color-violation-red for the indicator line
    68→</constraints>
    69→<output>
    70→  RedLineTooltip.tsx component; ViolationOverlay hook that maps backend JSON to
    71→  VexFlow coordinate positions
    72→</output>
    73→```
    74→
    75→**Meta-Rewrite:**
    76→- What is the user asking for? Inline violation rendering on the score canvas.
    77→- Minimum surface area: one component + one coordinate-mapping hook. No store changes needed if violation flags are already in Zustand.
    78→- Ambiguities: What is the shape of the backend violation JSON? Need `{ measure, beat, voices, ruleLabel, definition }` at minimum.
    79→
    80→**Shall I proceed with the implementation as described above? (Yes / No / Request changes)**
    81→
    82→---
    83→
    84→## Example 3 — Protocol / Non-Code Request
    85→
    86→**User says:** "Explain what a deceptive cadence is."
    87→
    88→**Correct preview response:**
    89→
    90→```xml
    91→<context>
    92→  Superman is asking for a music theory explanation, likely for context before
    93→  implementing a Theory Inspector feature that detects or displays cadence types.
    94→</context>
    95→<task>
    96→  Define a deceptive cadence using the Theory Named strategy, anchored in academic
    97→  sources, and explain its relevance to the SATB constraint-satisfaction context.
    98→</task>
    99→<constraints>
   100→  - Theory Named strategy required: every claim must be anchored in an academic definition
   101→  - No probabilistic guessing — if uncertain about a specific case, state the uncertainty
   102→  - No frontend code generated unless explicitly requested
   103→</constraints>
   104→<output>
   105→  A concise academic definition with source citation and one practical SATB example
   106→</output>
   107→```
   108→
   109→**Meta-Rewrite:**
   110→- What is the user asking for? A rigorous music theory definition, not an implementation.
   111→- Minimum surface area: definition + citation + one example. No code.
   112→- Ambiguities: None — this is a pure explanation task.
   113→
   114→**Shall I proceed with the implementation as described above? (Yes / No / Request changes)**
   115→
   116→---
   117→
   118→## Anti-Patterns (what NOT to do)
   119→
   120→**Do not blend preview and execution:**
   121→
   122→```
   123→# WRONG — preview and code in the same message
   124→Here's how I'll implement it:
   125→<context>...</context>
   126→...
   127→Here's the code:
   128→const PlaybackButton = () => { ... }
   129→```
   130→
   131→**Do not skip the gate:**
   132→
   133→```
   134→# WRONG — no confirmation question at the end
   135→<context>...</context>
   136→<task>...</task>
   137→...
   138→I'll now write the component.
   139→```
   140→
   141→**Do not use plain text instead of XML tags:**
   142→
   143→```
   144→# WRONG — plain text format (pre-upgrade format)
   145→context: ...
   146→task: ...
   147→constraints: ...
   148→output: ...
   149→```
   150→
```
</details></tool-use>

### 2. .claude/ Directory Structure (Complete)

**Top-level files:**
- `.claude/settings.json` — Main Claude Code configuration (hooks, permissions, attribution, claude-flow v3 settings)
- `.claude/settings.local.json` — Local overrides (enables MCP servers)
- `.claude/memory.db` — Persistent memory database

**Subdirectories:**

#### `.claude/agents/` (117 files)
Organized by agent type:
- **core/** — `coder.md`, `planner.md`, `researcher.md`, `reviewer.md`, `tester.md`
- **v3/** — 13 v3-specific agents (ddd-domain-expert, security-architect, memory-specialist, etc.)
- **swarm/** — Coordinator agents (hierarchical, mesh, adaptive)
- **sparc/** — SPARC methodology agents (architecture, pseudocode, specification, refinement)
- **github/** — 14 GitHub-related agents (pr-manager, code-review-swarm, issue-tracker, etc.)
- **development/** — Backend API development agents
- **devops/** — CI/CD agents
- **testing/** — TDD and validation agents
- **analysis/** — Code quality and review agents
- **consensus/** — Byzantine fault tolerance, CRDT, Raft, quorum management
- **optimization/** — Performance tuning agents
- **data/** — ML/data model agents
- **documentation/** — API documentation agents
- **flow-nexus/** — Advanced features (app-store, authentication, payments, sandbox, swarm)
- **specialized/** — Mobile/React Native agents
- **templates/** — Template generators and base templates
- **other:** goal, payments, sona (learning optimizer), sublinear (matrix, pagerank, consensus)

#### `.claude/commands/` (159 files)
Command definitions organized by category:
- **sparc/** — 32 SPARC workflow commands (code, debug, designer, devops, documenter, innovator, integration, mcp, memory-manager, optimizer, orchestrator, researcher, reviewer, security-review, tester, workflow-manager, etc.)
- **github/** — 21 GitHub commands (code-review-swarm, issue-tracker, pr-manager, release-manager, repo-architect, sync-coordinator, workflow-automation, etc.)
- **automation/** — 6 smart automation commands (auto-agent, self-healing, session-memory, smart-spawn, workflow-select)
- **analysis/** — 5 performance analysis commands (bottleneck-detect, token-efficiency, performance-report, compliance-report)
- **optimization/** — 5 optimization commands (auto-topology, cache-manage, parallel-execution, topology-optimize)
- **monitoring/** — 5 monitoring commands (agent-metrics, real-time-view, status, swarm-monitor)
- **hooks/** — 7 hook lifecycle commands (pre-edit, post-edit, pre-task, post-task, session-end, overview, setup)
- **Root level:** claude-flow-help, claude-flow-memory, claude-flow-swarm

#### `.claude/rules/` (5 files)
**Core project rules:**
1. **hci-protocol.md** — Interaction workflow
   - Mandatory preview → gate → execute pattern
   - XML-structured blocks for requests
   - Confirmation gate enforcement
   - Vague request clarification protocol (4-question scaffold)

2. **music-theory.md** — Music theory constraints
   - Theory Named strategy (academic anchoring required)
   - Intrinsic Determinism boundary (backend vs frontend)
   - SATB constraints reference
   - Red Line violation rendering rules

3. **typescript-patterns.md** — Code style and architecture
   - Tech stack: Next.js, Tailwind, VexFlow, Tone.js, Zustand
   - No `any` types, strict TypeScript
   - POUR accessibility requirements
   - Design token locking (Nocturne/Sonata only)
   - ui-ux-pro-max suggestion protocol

4. **output-completeness.md** — Code delivery quality
   - Bans partial code (`// ...`, `TODO`, etc.)
   - Bans prose shortcuts
   - Full deliverable requirement
   - Token limit handling with pause/resume

5. **examples.md** — Few-shot examples
   - 3 detailed examples (UI component, Theory Inspector, protocol request)
   - Anti-patterns guide

#### `.claude/skills/` (25 SKILL.md files + data)
Persistent skill definitions:
- **swarm/** — `swarm-orchestration.md`, `swarm-advanced.md`, `v3-swarm-coordination.md`
- **memory/** — `agentdb-memory-patterns.md`, `agentdb-vector-search.md`, `agentdb-advanced.md`, `agentdb-optimization.md`, `agentdb-learning.md`
- **architecture/** — `v3-ddd-architecture.md`, `v3-integration-deep.md`
- **github/** — Multiple repo management and workflow skills
- **ui-ux/** — `ui-ux-pro-max/` with 17 CSV data files (colors, typography, stacks, components, etc.) + scripts
- **pair-programming/** — Collaborative coding skill
- **sparc-methodology/** — SPARC workflow coordination
- **verification-quality/** — Testing and QA
- **v3 skills:** cli-modernization, core-implementation, mcp-optimization, memory-unification, performance-optimization, security-overhaul

Data files in `ui-ux-pro-max/data/`:
- colors.csv, typography.csv, styles.csv, icons.csv, charts.csv
- Stack-specific CSVs: nextjs, react, vue, svelte, nuxt, astro, tailwind, flutter, react-native, etc.
- UX reasoning and web interface guidelines

#### `.claude/helpers/` (47 files)
Script utilities and hooks:
- **auto-memory-hook.mjs** — Memory synchronization
- **hook-handler.cjs** — Lifecycle hook orchestration
- **statusline.cjs/.js** — Status bar display

## FIC Integration Plan for HarmonyForge

### What Is Actually Being Designed

Three slash-command files implementing Frequent Intentional Compaction (a workflow where each phase is bounded by an intentional `/compact` call so the next phase begins with a clean, dense context rather than a degraded one), plus the artifact directory structure and a CLAUDE.md documentation section.

---

## 1. Directory Structure

```
.claude/
  commands/
    compaction/
      research_codebase.md    ← /research_codebase
      create_plan.md          ← /create_plan
      implement_plan.md       ← /implement_plan
  thoughts/
    shared/
      research/               ← timestamped research artifacts land here
      plans/                  ← plan artifacts land here
```

The `thoughts/` tree is new and must be created. It is inside `.claude/` to satisfy the "NEVER save to root folder" rule. The `compaction/` subfolder matches the existing category-per-folder pattern (`sparc/`, `github/`, `automation/`, etc.).

---

## 2. Full File Contents

### File 1: `.claude/commands/compaction/research_codebase.md`

```markdown
# FIC: Research Codebase

## Purpose

Phase 1 of the Frequent Intentional Compaction (FIC) workflow.

Conduct a thorough, subagent-driven exploration of the codebase for the given
topic. Produce a structured research artifact that survives context compaction.
After this command completes, run `/compact` so that Phase 2 (`/create_plan`)
begins with a dense, clean context rather than a full conversation history.

## Usage

```
/research_codebase <topic-or-feature-description>
```

**Example:**

```
/research_codebase RedLine tooltip rendering for parallel-fifth violations
```

## Artifact Location

Artifacts are written to:

```
.claude/thoughts/shared/research/YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM-SS_<slug>.md
```

The timestamp prefix guarantees uniqueness and allows multiple research passes
for the same topic to coexist without overwriting.

## Instructions

When this command is invoked, execute the following procedure exactly.

**Step 0 — Antigravity skill search (MANDATORY)**

Before any exploration, run:

```bash
python3 references/antigravity-awesome-skills/scripts/search.py "$ARGUMENTS" --limit 5
```

Read the top matching SKILL.md. Apply its constraints throughout the research.

**Step 1 — Spawn parallel research subagents**

Spawn all of the following subagents in a single message using `run_in_background: true`.
Do not check status after spawning — wait for results.

- **Agent A — Architecture Surveyor**: Map top-level directory structure,
  identify all modules related to the topic `$ARGUMENTS`, trace import graphs
  and Zustand store slices that touch the feature area. Output: component tree
  and data-flow summary.

- **Agent B — Pattern Miner**: Use Glob and Grep to find all existing
  implementations, type definitions, hooks, and utility functions relevant to
  `$ARGUMENTS`. Identify naming conventions, file-naming patterns, and token
  usage from `src/app/globals.css`. Output: pattern inventory with file paths
  and line references.

- **Agent C — Constraint Auditor**: Read `.claude/rules/music-theory.md`,
  `.claude/rules/typescript-patterns.md`, `.claude/rules/hci-protocol.md`,
  and `CLAUDE.md`. Extract every constraint that applies to the feature area
  described in `$ARGUMENTS`. Output: constraint checklist with rule source
  citations.

- **Agent D — Test and Build Scanner**: Scan `/tests`, check `package.json`
  scripts, and identify existing test coverage gaps for the topic area.
  Output: test coverage map and build dependency notes.

**Step 2 — Collect and synthesize**

After all four agents return, synthesize their outputs. Do not discard any
finding — completeness is required (see `.claude/rules/output-completeness.md`).

**Step 3 — Write the research artifact**

Compute the timestamp: `date +"%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M-%S"`. Derive a kebab-case slug
from `$ARGUMENTS` (e.g., `red-line-tooltip-parallel-fifth`). Write the artifact
to:

```
.claude/thoughts/shared/research/YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM-SS_<slug>.md
```

The artifact must use this exact structure:

```markdown
# Research: <topic>

**Date:** YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS
**Topic:** <$ARGUMENTS verbatim>
**Skill applied:** <name of Antigravity skill read in Step 0>

## Architecture Map
<Agent A output>

## Pattern Inventory
<Agent B output — file paths and line references required>

## Constraint Checklist
<Agent C output — cite rule source files>

## Test Coverage Map
<Agent D output>

## Synthesis — Open Questions
<List any ambiguities or gaps that the create_plan phase must resolve>

## Recommended Next Step
Run `/create_plan <topic>` and reference this file at:
`.claude/thoughts/shared/research/YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM-SS_<slug>.md`
```

**Step 4 — Emit the compaction prompt**

After writing the artifact, output exactly this block and nothing else:

```
Research artifact written to:
  .claude/thoughts/shared/research/YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM-SS_<slug>.md

Next step: run /compact, then run:
  /create_plan <topic>
```

## FIC Rationale

Running `/compact` after research discards conversation noise and compresses
context. The research artifact is the durable, compaction-safe knowledge base.
`/create_plan` reads the artifact file directly — it does not depend on
conversation history — so the plan is built from verified findings, not from
what survived the context window.
```

---

### File 2: `.claude/commands/compaction/create_plan.md`

```markdown
# FIC: Create Plan

## Purpose

Phase 2 of the Frequent Intentional Compaction (FIC) workflow.

Read the research artifact produced by `/research_codebase` and produce a
concrete, phase-gated implementation plan with exact file paths, code surface
area, and verification steps. After this command completes, run `/compact` so
that Phase 3 (`/implement_plan`) begins with only the plan file in context.

## Usage

```
/create_plan <topic-or-feature-description>
```

**Example:**

```
/create_plan RedLine tooltip rendering for parallel-fifth violations
```

The topic must match the topic used in the preceding `/research_codebase` call.
This command will locate the most recent matching research artifact automatically.

## Artifact Location

Plans are written to:

```
.claude/thoughts/shared/plans/<kebab-slug>.md
```

If a plan for this slug already exists, it is overwritten with the updated plan.
(Plans are idempotent — re-running `create_plan` for the same topic regenerates
from the latest research artifact.)

## Instructions

When this command is invoked, execute the following procedure exactly.

**Step 0 — Antigravity skill search (MANDATORY)**

Before any planning, run:

```bash
python3 references/antigravity-awesome-skills/scripts/search.py "$ARGUMENTS" --limit 5
```

Read the top matching SKILL.md. Apply its constraints to the plan structure.

**Step 1 — Locate the research artifact**

Search `.claude/thoughts/shared/research/` for the most recent file whose name
contains the kebab slug derived from `$ARGUMENTS`. If no match is found, halt
and output:

```
No research artifact found for: $ARGUMENTS
Run /research_codebase <topic> first, then /compact, then retry /create_plan.
```

Read the located artifact completely before proceeding.

**Step 2 — Resolve open questions**

The research artifact contains a "Synthesis — Open Questions" section. For each
open question, either:

- Answer it from the artifact's own evidence (Pattern Inventory, Architecture Map), or
- Mark it as a constraint assumption with a stated risk.

Do not defer open questions silently — every one must be addressed before the
plan is written.

**Step 3 — Draft the implementation plan**

Using the HCI protocol preview format, structure the plan as follows:

```xml
<context>...</context>
<task>...</task>
<constraints>...</constraints>
<output>...</output>
```

Immediately after the XML block, produce a phased implementation plan with this
exact structure per phase:

```
### Phase N — <Phase Name>

**Files to create or modify:**
- `<exact/path/to/file.ts>` — <one-line description of change>

**Implementation steps:**
1. <Concrete step with exact function names, type names, or token names>
2. ...

**Verification:**
- [ ] `npm run build` passes
- [ ] `npm run lint` passes
- [ ] <Feature-specific check — e.g., "RedLine tooltip renders on beat 3, measure 5">

**Compaction point:** After Phase N is complete, run `/compact` and then
`/implement_plan <topic> phase:<N+1>`.
```

Constraints on the plan:
- Every file path must be absolute from the repository root (e.g., `src/components/canvas/RedLineTooltip.tsx`).
- Every phase ends with a compaction point instruction.
- No phase may exceed 4 files modified or created (to respect the "one task per turn" and "lost in the middle" guidance in CLAUDE.md).
- The Antigravity skill constraint and HarmonyForge design system tokens must appear in the `<constraints>` block.
- The plan must cite the research artifact by path in its header.

**Step 4 — Write the plan artifact**

Write the complete plan to:

```
.claude/thoughts/shared/plans/<kebab-slug>.md
```

The plan file must begin with:

```markdown
# Plan: <topic>

**Research artifact:** `.claude/thoughts/shared/research/YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM-SS_<slug>.md`
**Created:** YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS
**Skill applied:** <name of Antigravity skill>
**Status:** READY — Phase 1 not started
```

**Step 5 — Emit the compaction prompt**

After writing the plan, output exactly this block and nothing else:

```
Plan written to:
  .claude/thoughts/shared/plans/<slug>.md

Next step: run /compact, then run:
  /implement_plan <topic> phase:1
```

## FIC Rationale

The plan file is the compaction-safe contract between planning and execution.
Phase-gated compaction means each implementation phase starts with only the
plan (dense, structured) in context — not the full planning conversation.
This prevents the "lost in the middle" degradation that occurs when
implementation spans many turns in one context window.
```

---

### File 3: `.claude/commands/compaction/implement_plan.md`

```markdown
# FIC: Implement Plan

## Purpose

Phase 3 of the Frequent Intentional Compaction (FIC) workflow.

Read the plan artifact produced by `/create_plan`, execute one phase, run
verification, and then prompt for compaction before the next phase. Each
invocation executes exactly one plan phase.

## Usage

```
/implement_plan <topic-or-feature-description> phase:<N>
```

**Examples:**

```
/implement_plan RedLine tooltip rendering for parallel-fifth violations phase:1
/implement_plan RedLine tooltip rendering for parallel-fifth violations phase:2
```

The `phase:<N>` argument is required. Omitting it is an error — output:

```
Error: phase number required.
Usage: /implement_plan <topic> phase:<N>
```

## Instructions

When this command is invoked, execute the following procedure exactly.

**Step 0 — Antigravity skill search (MANDATORY)**

Before any implementation, run:

```bash
python3 references/antigravity-awesome-skills/scripts/search.py "$ARGUMENTS" --limit 5
```

Read the top matching SKILL.md. Apply its implementation constraints throughout
this phase.

**Step 1 — Locate and read the plan artifact**

Derive the kebab slug from the topic portion of `$ARGUMENTS` (everything before
`phase:`). Search for:

```
.claude/thoughts/shared/plans/<slug>.md
```

If not found, halt and output:

```
No plan artifact found for: <topic>
Run /create_plan <topic> first, then /compact, then retry /implement_plan.
```

Read the plan artifact completely. Extract the specified phase block.

**Step 2 — Confirm phase scope**

Before writing any code, output a brief scope statement:

```
Executing Phase <N>: <Phase Name>
Files in scope: <list exact paths>
Verification steps: <list checks>
Compaction point follows Phase <N>.
```

Pause here. If the scope is incorrect, the user can abort. If the user confirms
or does not object, proceed to Step 3.

**Step 3 — Execute the phase**

Implement every step listed in the plan's Phase N block. Rules:

- ALWAYS read each target file completely before editing it (CLAUDE.md mandate).
- NEVER introduce ad-hoc hex colors or Tailwind overrides outside Nocturne/Sonata tokens.
- NEVER implement constraint-satisfaction logic in the frontend — backend only.
- ALL VexFlow DOM manipulation must occur inside `useEffect` only.
- ALL Zustand store changes must be typed — no implicit `any`.
- NEVER truncate code with `// ...` or `// TODO` shortcuts.
- If any ambiguity arises mid-phase, halt and output an explicit uncertainty
  statement: "I don't know [X]. To resolve this I need [Y]." Do not guess.

**Step 4 — Run verification**

After implementation, run each verification step from the plan:

```bash
npm run build
npm run lint
```

If either fails, do not proceed to the compaction prompt. Fix the failure first,
then re-run verification. A phase is not complete until all checks pass.

**Step 5 — Update the plan artifact status**

Open `.claude/thoughts/shared/plans/<slug>.md`. Update the status line:

```markdown
**Status:** Phase <N> COMPLETE — Phase <N+1> not started
```

If this was the final phase, write:

```markdown
**Status:** COMPLETE — all phases implemented
```

**Step 6 — Emit the compaction prompt**

If more phases remain, output exactly this block:

```
Phase <N> complete. Verification passed.
Plan updated: .claude/thoughts/shared/plans/<slug>.md

Next step: run /compact, then run:
  /implement_plan <topic> phase:<N+1>
```

If this was the final phase, output:

```
Phase <N> complete. Verification passed.
All phases implemented.
Plan updated: .claude/thoughts/shared/plans/<slug>.md

Implementation complete. Review the plan artifact for a full audit trail.
```

## FIC Rationale

Each phase ends with an explicit compaction instruction. The plan artifact is
the authoritative state tracker — it survives every `/compact` call. When the
next `/implement_plan phase:<N+1>` runs, it reads the plan file to know exactly
where execution left off. The conversation history is irrelevant; the plan file
is the contract.

## Architecture Boundary Reminder

This command executes frontend code only. Do not implement:
- Constraint-satisfaction logic (backend TypeScript Logic Core)
- LLM Theory Inspector API calls (backend service)
- Raw AudioContext or audio buffer manipulation (Tone.js symbolic scheduling only)

If a plan phase appears to require backend logic, halt and flag it as an
architecture boundary violation before proceeding.
```

---

## 3. CLAUDE.md Addition

The following section appends to the end of `/Users/shivpat/harmony-forge-redesign/CLAUDE.md`, after the existing `## Support` section.

```markdown
## Frequent Intentional Compaction (FIC) Workflow

FIC is a three-phase workflow for complex, multi-turn implementation tasks. Each
phase ends with an explicit `/compact` call, so the next phase begins with a
dense, structured context (the artifact file) rather than a degraded conversation
history. Use FIC for any task that spans more than one session or more than ~4
subtasks.

### When to Use FIC

- Feature implementation that touches more than 3 files
- Bug investigations that require codebase exploration before a fix
- Refactors that need a plan before any code is written
- Any task where "lost in the middle" degradation (Liu et al., 2023) is a risk

### The Three Commands

| Command | Purpose | Run `/compact` after? |
|---|---|---|
| `/research_codebase <topic>` | Explore codebase; write research artifact | Yes — always |
| `/create_plan <topic>` | Read research; write phased plan artifact | Yes — always |
| `/implement_plan <topic> phase:<N>` | Execute one plan phase; verify; update artifact | Yes — if more phases remain |

### Artifact Locations

All FIC artifacts live inside `.claude/thoughts/shared/` — never in the root
folder.

```
.claude/thoughts/shared/
  research/   ← YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM-SS_<slug>.md  (one per research pass)
  plans/      ← <slug>.md  (one per topic, overwritten on re-plan)
```

The timestamp-prefixed research files and the plan files are the compaction-safe
knowledge base. They persist across sessions. Read them directly at the start of
any resumed session rather than relying on conversation history.

### Standard FIC Sequence

```
1. /research_codebase <topic>
2. /compact
3. /create_plan <topic>
4. /compact
5. /implement_plan <topic> phase:1
6. /compact
7. /implement_plan <topic> phase:2
8. /compact
   ... (repeat for each phase)
```

### Integration with HCI Protocol

FIC does not bypass the preview → gate → execute workflow. The `/create_plan`
command produces the XML structured block (`<context>`, `<task>`, `<constraints>`,
`<output>`) and ends with the confirmation gate before writing any plan.
`/implement_plan` emits a scope statement and pauses before executing. The
confirmation gate is the final line of any preview produced within a FIC command.

### Integration with Antigravity Skills

All three FIC commands begin with a mandatory Antigravity skill search (step 0).
The skill name is recorded in every artifact header so that the constraint source
is auditable across sessions.
```

---

## Summary of All Files to Create

| Path | Status |
|---|---|
| `.claude/commands/compaction/research_codebase.md` | New file |
| `.claude/commands/compaction/create_plan.md` | New file |
| `.claude/commands/compaction/implement_plan.md` | New file |
| `.claude/thoughts/shared/research/` | New directory (empty, created by first research run) |
| `.claude/thoughts/shared/plans/` | New directory (empty, created by first plan run) |
| `CLAUDE.md` | Append one new section at end |

Note: The two `thoughts/` subdirectories do not need to be pre-created — they are created implicitly when the first artifact is written. If the filesystem requires them to exist first, a single `mkdir -p .claude/thoughts/shared/research .claude/thoughts/shared/plans` in a setup script or the first run of `/research_codebase` resolves it.

---

## Key Design Decisions and Trade-offs

**Why `compaction/` as its own subfolder rather than `sparc/`?**
The SPARC methodology commands are agent-execution commands tied to the claude-flow MCP protocol. FIC is a workflow protocol — it governs when to compact, not which agent to spawn. Mixing them would obscure the categorical boundary. A dedicated `compaction/` folder makes the workflow findable and correctly scoped.

**Why plain prose markdown style (no `---` frontmatter)?**
The agent-definition hybrid files in `sparc/` use frontmatter because they are parsed by the claude-flow runtime as agent specs. FIC commands are slash-commands consumed by Claude Code's slash-command reader, not the claude-flow agent spawner. Using plain prose matches `automation/`, `analysis/`, and `monitoring/` — the correct reference class.

**Why is the plan file overwritten rather than timestamped?**
Research artifacts are timestamped because multiple research passes over the same topic are useful (each pass may target a different angle). Plans are idempotent by intent — re-running `/create_plan` for a topic should replace the previous plan with an updated one that reflects any new research. Having two conflicting plan files for the same slug would force `/implement_plan` to guess which is authoritative.

**Why does `/implement_plan` pause after the scope statement?**
This is a lightweight confirmation gate that mirrors the HCI protocol without requiring a full XML structured block (which would be redundant — the plan already contains the structured block from `/create_plan`). It gives Superman one last check before any file is modified.

**Why cap each phase at 4 files?**
CLAUDE.md cites Liu et al. (2023) on "lost in the middle" degradation and states "one task per turn" for compound requests. Four files per phase is a practical bound that keeps each `/implement_plan` invocation within a single coherent attention scope while leaving room for the plan file itself to be read and updated.

---

### Critical Files for Implementation

- `/Users/shivpat/harmony-forge-redesign/.claude/commands/sparc/orchestrator.md` - Pattern to follow: the format of workflow-style slash commands that coordinate subagents
- `/Users/shivpat/harmony-forge-redesign/.claude/commands/automation/auto-agent.md` - Pattern to follow: plain prose markdown style (no frontmatter) used by non-SPARC command files
- `/Users/shivpat/harmony-forge-redesign/CLAUDE.md` - File to append to: the root behavioral rules file that documents the FIC workflow for all sessions
- `/Users/shivpat/harmony-forge-redesign/.claude/agents/core/researcher.md` - Reference: the subagent capability model that research_codebase.md's Agent A/B/C/D calls into
- `/Users/shivpat/harmony-forge-redesign/.claude/rules/hci-protocol.md` - Constraint source: the preview-gate-execute protocol that create_plan.md must embed in its plan output

Design a detailed integration plan for adding "Frequent Intentional Compaction" (FIC) commands to an existing Claude Code project.

## Background

The HarmonyForge repository already has:
- `.claude/commands/` with 159 commands organized by category (sparc/, github/, automation/, analysis/, optimization/, monitoring/, hooks/)
- `.claude/rules/` with 5 rule files (hci-protocol.md, music-theory.md, typescript-patterns.md, output-completeness.md, examples.md)
- `.claude/agents/` with 117 agent definitions
- `CLAUDE.md` at root with all behavioral rules including:
  - Skill trigger protocol
  - Antigravity skills search requirement
  - Swarm orchestration rules
  - "NEVER save working files, text/mds, or tests to the root folder" rule
  - Preview → gate → execute HCI protocol

## What to Add

The article "Getting AI to Work in Complex Codebases" describes a **Frequent Intentional Compaction** workflow with three command files:
1. `research_codebase.md` - Subagent-driven codebase exploration, produces a structured research artifact
2. `create_plan.md` - References research to create a concrete implementation plan with exact file paths and verification steps
3. `implement_plan.md` - Executes plan phase by phase with compaction back to plan file between phases

The article's example artifact paths are: `thoughts/shared/research/YYYY-MM-DD_HH-MM-SS_topic.md` and `thoughts/shared/plans/plan-name.md`

## Constraints

1. Respect the "NEVER save to root folder" rule → artifacts go in `.claude/thoughts/`
2. Must fit the existing preview → gate → execute HCI protocol (CLAUDE.md)
3. Must use existing command organization patterns (add a new `compaction/` subfolder)
4. Commands must be slash-command compatible (Claude Code `/command-name` syntax)
5. Must use the existing Antigravity skill search pattern before any non-trivial work

## Design Task

Please design:

1. **Directory structure** - Exact paths for the 3 new command files and the thoughts artifact directory
2. **Full content** for each of the 3 command files - these are markdown slash-command definitions that tell Claude how to execute the workflow step. They should:
   - Use the `$ARGUMENTS` variable for user-provided topic/feature/bug description
   - Use the Task() tool pattern for subagents (as described in CLAUDE.md)
   - Use `run_in_background: true` for agents
   - Store artifacts with timestamp-prefixed names
   - Reference `.claude/thoughts/shared/` as the artifact store
3. **CLAUDE.md additions** - A new section to add to the root CLAUDE.md documenting the FIC workflow commands, where artifacts live, and when to use each step

The command files should match the style of the existing `.claude/commands/` markdown format: plain markdown with a description, usage pattern, and the actual prompt/instructions to follow.

Return:
- Exact file paths
- Full content for each file
- The exact CLAUDE.md addition (section to append)
